speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three. Today's date is November 26, 1995. And this is an interview with Clara Posner. You were telling me about how your family was in the woods for three months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, only the-- the brothers and the sister. Not my mother. I told you. They shot-- killed my mother with the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who killed your mother and your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they took them away, the Germans! The Germans or maybe the-- the Ukrainian police. Who knows? Why didn't you give Bill something? [POINTS] Oh, I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] How is that?

speaker: interviewer
text: --where did-- where did they take them? Where did they take your mother and--

speaker: interviewee
text: To schiessen, to shot. [LAUGHS] Where did they take them?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever-- what happened to them there? What happened to your mother and your baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. I saw them geschossen. [INAUDIBLE] I never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your brothers and sisters escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But your mother and your baby were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the woods? You said you stole food--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to stay alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you slept on the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when the weather was-- was bad or cold? What-- what did you do on a daily basis--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, what we did. We just watching to-- to hear, to-- to-- to hear if somebody. When they came, they shot everybody. Only I escaped. I don't know. I run. There, everybody run when they start shooting. Everybody run, but they didn't shoot me

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: --find enough food--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait, I'll tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the woods? No. When my brothers were, he used to go in the village. He got people who he used to make business, they gave him. But when they shot him?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, when I escaped, when they start shootin', and they killed everybody, and I escaped-- I-- when I-- I escape, and there was snow. What? I don't, you know? And I been lying whole day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then at night, I came out. I see-- I-- I hear it's quiet. I figure, at night, nobody's there. I came out. And I start calling my sister, my brothers. I didn't hear nothing. I sat down, and I was sitting whole night, freezing like this. In the morning, the next day, I saw my husband and then another guy, a man from another city. He was there, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's all. And then, we escaped. We hiding in the woods. That's all. And I-- I never saw. And then, we went to the people. And they told us that everybody was-- only three of us alive. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that happen? Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, it would have been five, six days before Christmas, at '42, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of your brothers and sisters-- were-- were--

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister, I've got one.

speaker: interviewer
text: One sister, right. --were shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Killed. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you see this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know. I didn't see. How can-- I escape, too. I run away. I didn't saw. Then in a-- a few months, my husband and I, we went looking. We thought-- and we find-- we find there was, in the woods, was a lot of wolves, pigs, things like that. And they-- you know, they smelled. They took them out. They ate them. And I-- when we came in a few months, maybe half a year, we saw the bones only-- the bones. But I recognize my sister. Because, you know, it's a funny thing, the hair, I recognized on [? kopf. ?] The-- You know something, the [YIDDISH]-- I-- I'll tell you it in Yiddish. I don't know if you'll understand. The [SPEAKING YIDDISH] they-- they are different. They're not-- they're the same thing. I recognize the [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. That's my sister's. And the rest was the bones only. And then, my husband and I, we buried the bones. We took the-- the bones, and we dig a-- a-- a-- We went in a-- a guy, a Polish guy. And he gave us a shovel. And we made a big grave. And we put on all the bones. And that's all. That's nicer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where did you bury the bones?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the woods, in the same place--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where we find the bones. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't answer. All right, answer. Tell her, I can't come. Tell her the true, you, all right? And after-- after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, [? Lebel? ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: After this happened-- after this happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --how long were you in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: After your brothers and sisters were shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years? You were-- after your brothers and sisters were shot, you were in the woods two more years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah!

speaker: interviewer
text: Until what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1942. I came home 1940-- --44 in July. The Russian occupied our city that time. The war wasn't finished yet. No. But our city, the Russian occupied our city. But we was in the woods not so fast. Two years, we was in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was 1944. It was July. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I heard Russian songs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said-- we were three of us. I said, did you hear Russian songs, [? girls ?] [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said-- she know Russian songs-- maybe they were-- the Ukrainian police has Russian girls, and they sing. It was a whole day like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, we said, well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had a man. We went. We climb a tree. We want to see what is there. And we saw the Russian soldiers. And we came out like this. And we said, we are Jews. We are two years in the woods. And they told him. They did that. So they gave us food. And they say, go home!

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the woods for two years, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, wait, wait, wait, wait. I forgot. Two Russian soldiers came in the woods. They escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was-- when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- in '42. Or maybe that was in '43, I don't know. Two Russian soldiers came with the gun, you know? They said they escaped. They want to-- they want to-- I don't want to-- They told us something. And they came in the woods. And-- [LAUGHS] and there was-- there was-- the [NON-ENGLISH] was, you know, to the border, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we said, listen something. We are Jews. If you want to stay with us, stay. But throw away the guns. I don't-- we-- Not the guns, a rifle, you know? I said, we don't want any, you-- you know? I-- we don't want them. If you want to stay with us, stay. And they threw away their rifles. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 19-- I think it's [NON-ENGLISH], about 1943, these Russian, 1943, when they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what-- what were you doing in the woods? You said you were there two years--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, what we do--

speaker: interviewer
text: --after your brothers and sisters were killed--

speaker: interviewee
text: What, we do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiding!

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Where did you sleep at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: We sleeped. And the-- I-- the-- we two. But the Polish people, they gave us hay. You know what this mean? Two bundles. At night, we put the hay. And I-- I sleep in there, in the bundle, [LAUGHS] in the hay. But then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Out-- outdoors? Outdoors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ground. But then, it starts snowing. We made a little-- I-- how-- how do you call this? Like for the dogs, a little one, you know? You know what I-- And we covered this with trees. We-- we took everything what we can find in the woods. It's only for the snow not for the rain. The rain came in. But only for the snow. When it snowed, we put-- we went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with who in the woods? You were with--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my husband and another-- another man from our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Noach. ?] [? Noach. ?] The name was Noach [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: The last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They used to call him [? Noach ?] [? Tznius. ?] In Europe, they used to call after-- yeah, let's say my name is Chaya. My mom-- mother's name is Hannah. Chaya Hannah is, you know? They let-- they used to call him after his mother's name or the father's. [? Noach-- ?] [? Tznius, ?] that I remember. That's all. He's in Canada now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a friend or a relative?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, just from our city. He escaped the same thing, in the same woods, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people in the woods? Did you ever see anybody else besides the Russian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was. There was two sisters, two girls. I don't know. They was from the-- from some village. I don't know. But then, in 1943-- I think it was in 1943-- can't remember-- a man came with two, a-- a girl and a boy. The girl was, that time, maybe a nine-year-old, and the boy was two year older or something. And they was with us two days. And then Ukrainian came. They used to come every day, almost. Not every day, almost. They knew it, people hiding in the woods. They knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they start shooting. And they shot the father with the boy. And the little girl was alive. And I kept. I said, stay with me. What's going to happen with me? Stay with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a few weeks, and there was very cold. And we took her in the village. There was a old lady. We knew her. My husband knew her or something. I don't know. And-- and he-- we said to the lady, keep her over the winter. On summer, we'll take her back. Maybe the war will-- will be finished in the summer. But the war wasn't finished. We went. The-- the lady was holding the little girl. She was all right. I said, stay there. Stay there. You got what to eat and where to sleep. Stay. And she was there. And in 1944, when I came home, when-- I told you, when we came out, and we-- when the Russian occupied our city, we came home. And I was there with my husband. The other man was-- I don't know where he went. And there, one-- one Friday, a man from a village-- I didn't know him-- he came. He was with me over Shabbos. He was sleep in me. I gave him to eat. And I told him, he said, you know something-- to the man. He told me where-- what village he is. He said, in the same village, not far-- I told him where-- there is a little girl. Tell her to come to me. I want to-- oh. And he-- he brought her the next Shabbos, the next Friday. He brought the little girl to me. And she with me all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, when the war was finished, in 1945, I said to my husband, I don't want to be here by the Russian. I don't want to. And we went-- they told us, the Russian, [NON-ENGLISH] we can go over there. But, oh, we can be Russian citizen there, or we can go wherever we want to. [? We can. ?] And he said, I want to go to Poland. I have relatives there. It's a lie, I didn't. Because how do I say, I was afraid to-- to tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- and I said-- he said, no, that's not your girl. You're too young to have a girl 10 year old. I said, that's my sister's daughter. He said, no, you can't take her. She got to-- I said, I can't-- I wouldn't leave my sister's daughter by you. She's the only girl. She wasn't my sister's. She was-- And she said-- he said, if you want her, you can adopt her. I said, OK. And I paid something. I don't know how much I paid for that to happen. And I took her with me. And we went the next village, to Poland. That was the Polish Chelm. She was with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1945. And then, two men came from-- I think they came, they said, from Israel. I think so. And they said, whatever-- if you know orphans, where they haven't got parents and that, to bring them to Lodz kibbutz. There is a Lodz kibbutz. And they'll take them to Israel. And this is the way. You know, we was afraid. Because the Polish-- a lot of Polish soldiers came home from the war without a leg, without a arm. And they used to say, a-- a Jewish-- mentioned their gold. I said to my husband, you know, take the little-- take Feiga. Her name was Feiga. Take Feiga to Lodz kibbutz. Let her stay there. And she went. And then, a few weeks later, she sent me a letter in Polish. She called me Ciocia. It just mean 'aunt.' And [? Chayka-- ?] they called me [? Chayka. ?] Not Chaya, [? Chayka ?] they called. And we are going to Israel. I hope we, very soon, we'll-- we'll-- you'll come, too. And that's all. She's in Israel. I know she's in Israel. Yeah. She's in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to go back for a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know, go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for a moment to the woods. You were there for-- for two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that you slept on hay.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the winter, there was a little house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a house, we made a little-- what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: A shack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a shack?

speaker: interviewee
text: --like-- like a dog, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all. Only for the snow, when it's snowing. Not the rain. Rain was-- it came in. But the snow, that's all. It was cold! Terrible, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: --got food from--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I escaped-- Food? I told you, we used to steal. Some of us a little better. Some of, we used to steal when the growing, from the gardener-- gardeners, you know? They used to-- potatoes, carrots, something. Whatever we find, we took, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were afraid while you were in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I was afraid! They used to come-- come always, every day, looking for Jews. We used to hiding, you know? We used to run.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once, I remember, we-- there was deers. You know what a deer? [? With-- ?] my husband and I would be sitting. And we hear somebody go. You know, and the woods was, the-- you know, they gone-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the from the [NON-ENGLISH], how do you call this? Say it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Little things with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Yiddish, shall I say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, say it in Yiddish. In

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING YIDDISH] from the [SPEAKING YIDDISH], like woods, [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my [YIDDISH]. Somebody's coming. Somebody's coming. My husband and I, we-- we run, we run, quiet. And we know, we run, and somebody's after us, after us. And then, we run. We said, stop. Let me see what it is again. We hide. And there was a deer. A deer [LAUGHS] was after us! Yeah. Ah, what can I say? We-- One-- one time was very snow. And we used to be-- we-- we was afraid to go out to the village to steal a potato or something. I got a few potatoes. Once, I remember, it was so cold. I put them here. I was afraid the potatoes will freeze. I wasn't afraid for me, but for the potatoes. [LAUGHS] [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a snow. And we was afraid to go out to the village, because, when we go in snow, they used to, you know, when it come, the Ukrainian, they used to see, you know, the steps. You know? We was afraid. And we didn't got nothing to eat. Nothing at all. We went. We walked and walked and walked in the woods. We find a dead horse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A dead horse, I don't know how long. My husband got a little knife, and he start cutting the skin. And it was black. Who knows how long he was lying there dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband cut out a piece of meat. He said come, we're going to cook a piece of meat. I said, I wouldn't eat this meat! It's black! Look! He said, you will, you will, you will. You hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we start cooking the piece of meat. And the water was black and with-- with the fresh water, this and that. And we ate. We ate. You know something? Believe me or not, we ate the whole horse over the winter! Yes. Yes, I eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And another thing I'll tell you, I forgot to tell you. We knew the-- a-- a lady, a old lady. She got a pig. And she cook for the pig big potatoes. She cooked. She didn't cut or nothing. She was an old lady. She put potatoes with the peels, and she gave the pig. My husband saw. He said, come on. We'll go there. She gave, every night, the pig potatoes. And we went in that-- the pig, where the pig was. And I had a little sack. And my husband hold the pig. And I took all the potatoes, and we run away to the woods. We took from the pig-- from the pig's mouth! [LAUGHS] Believe me or not! [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I used to tell my friend, I used to eat from the pig's mouth. She said, how? I told her how. I said, my husband hold the pig, and I took the potatoes, and we run away. We got a few days to eat potatoes, cooked potatoes, with the peels, for the pigs. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We ate from the pig. We ate a-- a dead horse, everything, OK, until 1944, two years. We came home 1944 in July, I told you, when the Russian occupied there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you would survive? While you were in the woods, did you think you would survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They used to come to shoot, you know? I know, not far where we-- where we were, there was a boy. He was frozen to death. When I'll see the boy, I knew him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew him. I-- I didn't saw when he was dead. But a-- a Polish lady told me that he was frozen. He froze to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe. And I know the second name, [? Dubetski. ?] I knew him. He was on the same street living with a-- He was a younger from me, I think, maybe five, six years younger from me. Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was five younger from me, I think. He was, that time, I don't know. He was younger. I knew him. I knew him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he freeze to death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? What's-- he's-- he's froze to death! [LAUGHS] He was hiding. There was, you know, the-- they used to cut the grass. And they put a, you know-- I-- I don't know how to call this. And he used to sleep there. And-- and you know? And he froze there to death. The Polish people told me. Yeah. A lot of people froze to death. They couldn't survive in the woods. They used to run away, run out, schiessen, [? most ?] geschossen, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what would've happened if you had tried to leave and-- and stay in a town or a village?

speaker: interviewee
text: A village? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't stay in village?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody was allowed inside the village. Oh, no. Oh, no. They-- they knew what we steal potatoes. But they wouldn't let us in, in the house. No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they-- they were afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, if they caught us in their house, they shoot us, with them, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in the village-- in the villages, Germans or Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The most, Ukrainian. The most, Ukrainian. There was German, too, but the most Ukrainian police. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever-- after your brothers and sisters were killed, did you ever see them in the woods? Did you ever see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, I-- I saw the bones.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. After your brothers and sisters were killed, did you ever see the Ukrainians or the Germans in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hear them. We hear them. They used to come. They used to come schiessen and this, you know? But we used-- we used to hiding. Ah! Yeah. Yeah. Only three of us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what happened after you left the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came home? I knew what nobody was there. When I escaped, I knew what nobody was there. They verbrennt everything to [? broken. ?] But my grandfather's house-- he was a contractor, I told you. He built himself a beautiful house, a brick house. And a Polish family was there when I came home. When I came, and I went, right away, to my grandfather's, I saw the house is-- is there. And I came. And there was a Polish family. And there was-- I right away, I went to the attic, because I hide-- my sister and I-- we hide a-- a lot of stuff in the attic, there, in my grandfather's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where was your grandfather's house? Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to my mother's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same street where the ghetto was. Yeah. And she said to me, oh, now, I know it's your house, because you go right away in the attic looking for things. I said, I hide in there, but you took it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, no, I didn't took it. I said, how do you know, when I went to the attic and to looking for stuff? How do you know I was looking for stuff? How do you know? I hide a lot of stuff there, coats and this and that. I said, you took it out. She said, no. I said, how do you know there was stuff? [LAUGHS] You know what I mean? She went. Right away, I said, go. She left, right away. But this house was empty, nothing. We were sleeping on the floor. Who-- who is the family that was living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, a Polish family. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you-- when you came there with your husband, they left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told them to go. I said, it's my house. But then, the Russian, a few Russian, big ones, generals or something, came. It was a beautiful house. And they came and said, how do you-- how do I know it's your house? I said, it's my grandfather's house. He said, bring me two witnesses if it's-- if it's your grandfather's house. I said, OK. I'll bring you. There was a few Ukrainian old people. They knew me. They knew me. I went to them. And I took two witnesses. And they went with me. I told them, they want to check. They want to throw me out. They went. They know me. They knew my mother. And they signed that I'm a-- a granddaughter. When-- if I'm a granddaughter, I-- they have no right to-- to throw me out. And I've been there until the war finished. Your-- well, wait a minute. Now, this. They saw me. I was standing in there. At one time, a Russian soldier went. He see me standing and looking. You know, I was looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what do you seen? You-- for Russian, he said, you very young. You-- you can't-- is-- the war is not finish. Our-- our girls is in the war. The Russian girls said the same thing. Like they-- from-- like in Israel, they go in-- in the army. And they want me sent to the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went. There was-- we were two girls and two-- four of us where. And they want to take it away. And we went. We find somebody, a Russian man. He was Jewish. We knew he was Jewish. And he was-- he was, I think, like this. He got it. If-- if he wanted, he can take. And if we wanted-- we went to them-- to him. He was crying. They want to take it away. We-- I said, look my legs. It's swollen. I was two years in the woods. And then he said, make yourself older. And we [? do and do. ?] But he said, we make older-- for you older. But he said to us, but you've got to work for the-- for the Russian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And asked me what I can do. I said, I can-- I can do sewing. And he asked me, did you know how to do-- do-- can see-- sew for the Russian soldiers shirts and underwears? I said, yeah, I know. He said, and you can cut, you know, if I can [NON-ENGLISH]? I said, yeah, I know, because my mother teached me how. And I did it. And he said to me, if you-- If you'll do it-- he-- he gave me 10 Ukrainian girls to-- to do the work. And I only do the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what I mean? And I was working all the time there. And he said, if you'll make so much-- he-- he said, I'll bring you, every day, so much and so much material, [NON-ENGLISH]. And you got to make so much and so much shirts and-- and [NON-ENGLISH], you know? I said, OK, I'll do it. And I did it. He said-- How long did you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the-- all the time, from 1944. And the war wasn't finished until 1945. About, maybe eight, nine months, I don't know how much, how long, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, if you'll make so much and so much over the monat, we'll pay you 1,200 ruble. You know what this mean, ruble? Russian. I said, all right. After the monat, he came. And I show him how much I made. He said, OK, you made good. And he gave me a piece of paper to go there and there, and they'll pay you. I said, OK. I went and show them the paper and everything. He gave me 180 ruble. I said, they told me you had to give me 1,200. Why do you give me 180-- 80 ruble? He said, do you know it's a war? It's a war. We got to give food for the soldiers, dressing, ammunition, and this and that. I can't give 12. You got to pay for it. And he gave me 180 ruble. I said, what shall I do for 180 ruble? Give me a place where to-- OK? All right. Should I finish? Go ahead, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Give me a place where to buy a bread. He said, no. You can buy-- if Russia [NON-ENGLISH]-- matches-- matches, kerosene. I said, I don't need matches and kerosene! I need bread, butter, something! No, he said. OK. OK. They say to end it. I'll tell you later.
